locked_facts: Resonance occurs when X_L = X_C, i.e., ωL = 1/ωC
At resonance: current I_max = V/R (maximum current)
At resonance: phase angle φ = 0 (V and I in phase)
At resonance: V_L = V_C but they are 180° apart and cancel completely
Higher Q → sharper resonance curve → more selective circuit
truth_statements: At resonance X_L exactly equals X_C — they cancel in the impedance formula
Minimum impedance at resonance equals R — not zero
At resonance current is maximum, not voltage
The resonance frequency depends only on L and C — not on R
Higher R gives lower Q and broader (less sharp) resonance peak

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show the resonance curve (I vs f) and demonstrate the effect of changing R. High R gives a wide, flat peak. Low R gives a sharp, tall spike. The sharpness IS the Q factor — a high-Q radio tuner can select one station from thousands because its resonance curve is so narrow.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three curves on the same axes: high R (broad), medium R (moderate), low R (sharp spike). The resonant frequency f₀ is the same for all three. Only the shape changes.

simulation_states.label: I vs f curve — the resonance signature
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Graph of current amplitude I_m vs driving frequency f. A bell-shaped curve with a clear peak at f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)).
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Below f₀: circuit is capacitive, X_C > X_L. Above f₀: circuit is inductive, X_L > X_C. At f₀: they cancel, Z is minimum, I is maximum.

simulation_states.label: At resonance — X_L = X_C cancellation
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Frequency slider at f₀. Phasor diagram shows V_L and V_C as equal length arrows pointing in exactly opposite directions — they completely cancel. Only V_R remains. Z = R.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The 'resonance' is the L and C voltages fighting each other to a draw — completely cancelling. The source only needs to drive the resistance.

simulation_states.label: Q factor — sharpness of resonance
simulation_states.what_student_sees: R slider that changes the shape of the resonance curve while keeping f₀ fixed. High R → low Q → broad peak. Low R → high Q → sharp spike.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Q factor tells you how selective the circuit is. A radio tuner needs high Q to distinguish between stations 0.1 MHz apart.

simulation_states.label: Bandwidth — practical significance
simulation_states.what_student_sees: The resonance curve with the half-power points marked at I_max/√2. The frequency range between these points is the bandwidth Δf = R/(2πL).
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Bandwidth is the range of frequencies the circuit responds to significantly. Narrow bandwidth = high selectivity = high Q.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: At resonance, Z = 0 so current becomes infinite
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: At resonance, X_L and X_C cancel, but Z = √(R² + 0²) = R — not zero. R is always present. Current maximum I_max = V/R, which is finite.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the impedance formula at resonance: Z = √(R² + (X_L - X_C)²) = √(R² + 0) = R. Numerically: if R = 100Ω, Z = 100Ω at resonance. Current = V/100, not infinite.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: Changing the resistance changes the resonant frequency
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)) depends only on L and C — not on R. Changing R changes the Q factor and bandwidth but not the frequency at which resonance occurs.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show R slider moving. Watch the resonance curve change shape (broader or sharper) but the peak frequency stays fixed at f₀. Label f₀ explicitly and show it does not move.
